coredata.dc:title: Enhanced electrocatalytic performance of 2D Ni-MOF for ethanol oxidation reaction by loading carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: В© 2022 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chi, Xiaoyu
Gao, Lingling
Zhou, Wendi
Zhang, Yujuan
Hu, Tuoping
coredata.dc:description: Due to the scarcity of precious metals, it is urgently desired to develop novel non-precious metal catalysts for ethanol oxidation reactions (EOR) to improve the property of direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFCs), but it is still a huge challenge. Currently, nickel-based catalysts are known as one of the candidates to replace precious metal catalysts. Among them, nickel-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been treated as a new platform towards the development of electrocatalysts. Herein, a 2D Ni-MOF 1 was solvothermally synthesised, and combines with carbon dots (CDs) for enhancing the conductivity of MOF 1 to prepare finally MOF1-CDs@CC composites. The current density (j) of the optimal MOF1-CDs@CC toward EOR is 119В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2, which exceed those of the EOR catalysts previously reported. Meanwhile, the optimal MOF1-CDs@CC has low Tafel slope (93.9В вЂ‹mV decв€’1) and high exchange j value (2.06В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹10в€’5В вЂ‹AВ вЂ‹cmв€’2). The excellent performance of MOF1-CDs@CC is mainly ascribed to accelerating EtOH adsorption on the surface of the catalyst and charge transfer after the introduction of CDs. After 10000В вЂ‹s durability test, the retention rate of the j value of MOF1-CDs@CC is 90% of that (118В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2) of the original one, which indicates that MOF1-CDs@CC catalyst has good stability. So MOF1-CDs@CC is expected to be used as a cost-effective EOR electrocatalyst due to its simple preparation method, excellent activity and stability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ni-MOF
Ethanol oxidation reaction
Carbon dots
Electrocatalytic performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The morphology of samples. (a) SEM images of MOF1-CDs@CC, (b) SEMвЂ“EDS mappings of MOF1-CDs@CC; (cвЂ“d) TEM images of MOF1-CDs@CC.
XPS spectras of the MOF1-CDs@CC. (a) Survey spectrum; (b) Ni 2p spectra; (c) C 1s spectra; (d) O 1s spectra.

CV curves. (a) CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC at a scan rate of 10В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1 in 1В вЂ‹M KOH solution with and without EtOH; (b) CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC with different concentration KOH; (c) CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC with different concentration EtOH; (d) CV curves of bare CC, MOF1 and MOF1-CDs@CC in 1В вЂ‹M KOH+1В вЂ‹M EtOH.

Electrochemical performance. (a) and (b): CV curves of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC in 1В вЂ‹M KOH at different scan rates, respectively; (c) and (d): The plots of j vs ОЅ1/2 of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC; (e) CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC at different temperatures in 1В вЂ‹M KOH; (f) Arrhenius Plots at different potentials.

EOR activity of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC in 1В вЂ‹M KOH and 1В вЂ‹M EtOH alkaline solution. (a) EIS plots of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC; (b) and (c) are CV curves of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC were measured in the voltage range 1.2вЂ“1.28В вЂ‹V vs. RHE with a scan rate of 30, 40,50,60,70,80,90,100В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1 in 1В вЂ‹M KOH +1В вЂ‹M EtOH respectively; (d) Double layer capacitance (Cdl).

Electrochemical performance of MOF1-CDs@CC for EOR. (a)Tafel slope of samples; (b) Comparison of potential at different current density between MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC; (c) CA plot of MOF1-CDs@CC; (d) CV cycling stability test of MOF1-CDs@CC.
Comparison of different Ni-based electrocatalysis for EOR.
The apparent activation energy (Ea) at different potentials.

head.ce:title: Enhanced electrocatalytic performance of 2D Ni-MOF for ethanol oxidation reaction by loading carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, North University of China, Taiyuan, 030051, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jinzhong University, Jinzhong, 030619, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jinzhong University, Jinzhong, 030619, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the scarcity of precious metals, it is urgently desired to develop novel non-precious metal catalysts for ethanol oxidation reactions (EOR) to improve the property of direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFCs), but it is still a huge challenge. Currently, nickel-based catalysts are known as one of the candidates to replace precious metal catalysts. Among them, nickel-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been treated as a new platform towards the development of electrocatalysts. Herein, a 2D Ni-MOF 1 was solvothermally synthesised, and combines with carbon dots (CDs) for enhancing the conductivity of MOF 1 to prepare finally MOF1-CDs@CC composites. The current density (j) of the optimal MOF1-CDs@CC toward EOR is 119В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2, which exceed those of the EOR catalysts previously reported. Meanwhile, the optimal MOF1-CDs@CC has low Tafel slope (93.9В вЂ‹mV decв€’1) and high exchange j value (2.06В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹10в€’5В вЂ‹AВ вЂ‹cmв€’2). The excellent performance of MOF1-CDs@CC is mainly ascribed to accelerating EtOH adsorption on the surface of the catalyst and charge transfer after the introduction of CDs. After 10000В вЂ‹s durability test, the retention rate of the j value of MOF1-CDs@CC is 90% of that (118В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2) of the original one, which indicates that MOF1-CDs@CC catalyst has good stability. So MOF1-CDs@CC is expected to be used as a cost-effective EOR electrocatalyst due to its simple preparation method, excellent activity and stability.

MOF1-CDs@CC was constructed by the combination of MOF1 and carbon dots, which showed excellent activity and stability for the oxidation of EtOH.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The energy crisis and environmental issues resulted from the combustion of limited fossil fuels become increasingly serious. To produce energy in a clean and sustainable way, the development of suitable technology has become a research hotspot [1вЂ“3]. Fuel cells, as a type of green energy apparatus with almost no emission, have attracted much attention from researchers. Moreover, direct liquid fuel cells have become one of the research hotspots owing to their high energy/power density and environmental friendliness [4,5]. Among them, direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFCs) are considered to be a more promising energy device due to the easy availability and low toxicity of ethanol [6]. The anodic reaction is the key to the holistic performance of DEFCs. However, the performance of ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR) is related to the quality of the EOR catalysts. Therefore, the fabrication and development of catalysts with high activity and long-term stability are the key problems to be solved urgently [7вЂ“9]. At present, platinum and palladium based catalysts are generally considered to be the state-of-the-art electrocatalysts for EOR due to their excellent electrocatalytic activity [10вЂ“12]. For example, Yang etВ al. have synthesised an ultra-thin palladium nanosheet as an EOR catalyst with the peak current density (j) of 2145В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹mgв€’1 [13]. Wu etВ al. have fabricated graphene-nickel nitride as an EOR catalyst, which has the mass catalytic activity of 3499.5В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹mgв€’1 [14]. Feng etВ al. have constructed porous bimetallic PdNi catalysts by calcining PdNi-dimethylglyoxime, which shows high electrocatalytic performance with j value of 1498.9В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹mgв€’1 [15]. Meng etВ al. have reported that the hierarchical palladium-silver alloy nanosheet Pd7Ag3 NS/C was used as catalyst toward EOR and its peak j value is 9365.9В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹mgв€’1 [16]. Nevertheless, low abundance and high cost have prevented precious metals from the widespread commercial applications in DEFC. Hence, it is desired to fabricate cheap as well as high-performance EOR catalysts [17,18].

At present, transition metals, alloys or their compounds are widely considered as candidates to replace noble metal based catalysts due to their cost-effectiveness, resistance to CO poisoning, high activity and excellent stability, thus producing cheap and stable EOR catalysts [19]. Nickel-based materials have attracted widespread attention for scientists because of their low price, easy analysis of the reaction process, and can be modified by various methods to promote the transformation of Ni valence state, which provides more active sites. For instance, Falk Muench etВ al. have obtained nanostructured Ni and NiвЂ“B tubes by combination of the new polymer template and a borane-based plating reaction with the j value of 19.2В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 [20]. Zhou etВ al. reported a self-supported three-dimensional (3D) Ni aerogel as an EOR catalyst with the j of 27.6В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 in the electrolyte of 1.0В вЂ‹M KOH with 0.1В вЂ‹M EtOH [21]. Shi etВ al. have fabricated Ni nanorods/N-doped activated carbon EOR catalyst with the j value of 47.5В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 [22]. Although Ni based catalysts for EOR have been extensively reported, their structure and morphology are too unitary and far from meeting the requirements of the best catalyst. At present, researchers are working to develop Ni-based catalytic materials with hierarchical pores. Among them, Ni-based metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are one of the most popular materials.

The structural characteristics of metal organic frameworks (MOFs), such as ordering and easy tuning, make it a promising platform for constructing non-precious metal catalysts [23]. However, due to its inherent poor conductivity, there are few reports on the direct use of MOFs as an electrocatalyst [24]. To improve the conductivity of MOFs, the preferred method is to combine MOFs with some conductive materials to prepare composites, which not only retains the structural characteristics of MOFs, but also enhances the conductivity of the composite [25вЂ“27].

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of carbon nanomaterial, have been widely applied in various fields such as energy storage and conversion due to its advantages of quantum size, large specific surface area, high stability as well as high conductivity [28вЂ“30]. Herein, the composite catalysts were synthesised by combining MOF 1 with CDs through wetness impregnation method. Furthermore, the optimal catalyst was obtained through a series of optimization experiments and characterized by RXRD, XPS, SEM, TEM, Raman, TGA. And the electrocatalytic activities of the MOF1-CDs@CC toward EOR were also investigated.

Ni(NO3)2 6H2O, N,N-Dimethylacetamide (DMA), acetonitrile, citric acid (CA) and urea were purchased from Tianjin Da Mao. 2,5-di(3,5-dicarboxylhenyl) aniline (H4TPTA) and 1,1вЂ™-(1,4-Phenylene)bis[1H-benzimidazole] (1,4-bibb) were obtained from Jinan Henghua Chemical Co., Ltd.. All these reagents were directly used without any purification.

H4TPTA (0.004В вЂ‹mmol), Ni(NO3)2 6H2O (0.004В вЂ‹mmol), 1,4-bibb (0.008В вЂ‹mmol)was dissolved in a glass tube containing 1В вЂ‹mL equal volume of DMA and CH3CN mixed solution, which was sealed under vacuum and kept at 130В вЂ‹В°C for 50В вЂ‹h, and then naturally cooling to room temperature to collect atrovirens crystals of MOF 1 with the yield of 75% (According to H4TPTA). Calcd. (%) for C62H43Ni2N9O10: C: 62.45; H: 3.61; N: 10.58; O: 13.43; Ni: 9.93. Found (%): C: 62.52; H: 3.78; N: 10.32; O: 13.21; Ni: 10.17.

According to the previously reported method, the CDs were prepared with a little modification [31]. Briefly, 10В вЂ‹g CA and 10В вЂ‹g urea were added into a Teflon-lined autoclave containing 100В вЂ‹mL of deionized water, which was heated at 200В вЂ‹В°C for 5В вЂ‹h, cooled to room temperature, and filtered to obtain the supernatant. Finally, the supernatant was freeze-dried at в€’50В вЂ‹В°C for 10В вЂ‹h to obtain CDs.

MOF 1 powder and PVDF (m:mВ вЂ‹=В вЂ‹8:1) were dispersed in the solution containing H2O and EtOH (v:vВ вЂ‹=В вЂ‹1:1, 0.01В вЂ‹mL) and stirred until the solution changes into a paste, which was coated on CC and was vacuum dried at 60В вЂ‹В°C overnight to obtain MOF 1@CC. Finally, the MOF1@CC was soaked by aqueous solution containing CDs for 30В вЂ‹min to obtain MOF1-CDs@CC, which was heated at 100В вЂ‹В°C for 15В вЂ‹min.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Supra55) was used to investigate the morphology and structure of the samples. Transmission electron microscopy was conducted on JEOL-2100F instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS ESCALAB MKII) and the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) fixed on the SEM microscope were used to analyse the composition of samples. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Mettler-Toledo (TGA/DSC). Furthermore, XRD patterns were measured on a Bruker D8 diffractometer employing Mo-Ka radiation (kВ вЂ‹=В вЂ‹0.71076В вЂ‹Г…) in the 2Оё range of 10В°вЂ“90В°.

All electrochemical measurements were performed with a standard three-electrode system in CHI660E electrochemical workstation at 25В вЂ‹В°C, in which the working electrode, the counter electrode and the reference electrode were MOF1-CDs@CC, stainless-steel plate of 3В вЂ‹cmВ вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹4В вЂ‹cm and saturated calomel electrode (SCE), respectively. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were carried out between 0В вЂ‹V and 0.6В вЂ‹V vs. SCE at the scan rate of 10В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) polarization curves were measured at the scan rate (ОЅ) of 1В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1. Double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was obtained by the CV tests with different ОЅ values. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out under AC stimulus in amplitude of 5В вЂ‹mV in the frequency range of 1В вЂ‹HzвЂ“100В вЂ‹kHz. The durability test was conducted by chronoamperometry (CA) at a fixed voltage of 0.6В вЂ‹V.The SCE was calibrated to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) according to Eq. (1).
(1) ERHEВ вЂ‹=В вЂ‹ESCE+0.2412В вЂ‹+В вЂ‹0.0591В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹pH

X-ray single-crystal diffraction indicated MOF 1 presents a triclinic space group P-1. Ni2+ is six-coordinated with three O atoms of TPTA4в€’, two N atoms of 1,4-bbib and one water molecule, displaying a slightly distorted octahedron geometry (Fig.В S1).

Two-dimensional (2D) structures (Fig.В S2a) were formed by H4TPTA ligands and Ni2+ ions, which are finally constructed into 3D supramolecular frameworks by OвЂ“Hв‹ЇO hydrogen bonding interactions. And the final 3D supramolecular framework has a 1D channel constructed by Ni2+ ions and 1, 4-bibb linkers (Fig.В S2b).
The topology analysis reveals that the whole network of MOF 1 is represented as a 2-node (4, 4)-connected network of {4.64.8}2{42. 64} (Fig.В S3).

Thermal stability of MOF 1 was tested by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As can be obtained from Fig.В S4 that a first weight loss of 10.33% (calc. 10.49%) below 200В вЂ‹В°C belongs to the loss of a lattice H2O, a coordinated H2O and a lattice DMA. And the weight loss at 370В вЂ‹В°C is due to the collapse of the framework of MOF 1. As depicted in Fig.В S5, the main characteristic peaks of the simulated curve were basically consistent with the main characteristic peaks obtained from the experimental curve, indicating that the MOF 1 has good purity. As shown in Fig.В S6, the main characteristic peaks of MOF 1 can still be observed in the XRD pattern of MOF1-CDs@CC, which shows that the combination of CDs and MOF 1 didn't destroy the structure of MOF 1. Furthermore, the N2 gas absorption isotherms of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC were measured at 77В вЂ‹K. As shown in Fig.В S7, compared with MOF 1 (8.99В вЂ‹m2В вЂ‹gв€’1), the larger Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) specific surface area of MOF1-CDs@CC (29.91В вЂ‹m2В вЂ‹gв€’1) is attributed to the addition of CDs, which enhances the electron transfer rate of MOF1-CDs@CC, thereby accelerating EOR process.

The morphology and microstructure of MOF1-CDs@CC were measured by SEM and TEM. The SEM image of MOF1-CDs@CC with irregular and lumpy structure is shown in Fig.В 1 a. Besides, as shown in Fig.В 1b, the surface of MOF1-CDs@CC is mainly composed of C, N, O and Ni elements with the atomic ratio of 78.6:7.0:13.0:1.4 (C: N: O: Ni) (Fig.В S8). Furthermore, TEM analysis (Fig.В 1c and d) exhibits the solid surface of MOF1-CDs@CC is dotted with CDs nanospheres.

Fig.В S9 shows that MOF1-CDs@CC presents the D-band and G-band of CDs in the Raman spectrum. Meanwhile, the MOF1-CDs@CC exhibited blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp (Fig.В S10), which indicates that CDs and MOF 1 have successfully combined to form MOF1-CDs@CC.

XPS survey spectra of MOF1-CDs@CC show four characteristics peaks of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and Ni 2p, as shown in Fig.В 2 a. There are four peaks in the Ni 2p spectrum, that is, Ni2+ 2p3/2 (855.7В вЂ‹eV), Ni2+ 2p1/2 (861.9В вЂ‹eV) and two satellite peaks of Ni2+ 2p3/2 (873.8В вЂ‹eV) and Ni2+ 2p1/2 (880.1В вЂ‹eV), respectively (Fig.В 2b) [32]. Additionally, the peaks located at 286.35В вЂ‹eV, 288.35В вЂ‹eV and 284.8В вЂ‹eV (Fig.В 2c), are attributed to CвЂ“O, C=O CвЂ“C, which can be seen in the high resolution C 1s spectrum [33,34]. The peaks of O 1s situated at 530.1eV and 531.8В вЂ‹eV are ascribed to NiвЂ“O and CвЂ“O, respectively (Fig.В 2d) [35,36].

To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity of MOF1-CDs@CC, the CV tests were carried out in KOH solution (1.0В вЂ‹M) or KOH solution (1.0В вЂ‹M) with EtOH (1.0В вЂ‹M) and the ОЅ value is 10В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1. It can be seen from Fig.В 3 a that when the electrolyte is KOH solution (1.0В вЂ‹M), a pair of symmetrical negative and positive peaks can be observed between 1.52 and 1.15В вЂ‹V, which is ascribed to the Ni2+/Ni3+ redox couple [37]. Herein, NiOOH obtained by the oxidation of Ni2+ ions was served as active constituents toward the EOR [38,39]. It is worth mentioned that when the electrolyte of KOH (1.0В вЂ‹M)В вЂ‹+В вЂ‹EtOH (1.0В вЂ‹M) was again used, the peak of Ni2+/Ni3+ disappeared and the peak j value at 1.60В вЂ‹V is much higher than that of in the electrolyte without EtOH, which might be ascribed to the overlap of the EOR current and the OER current [40]. Meanwhile, this result also shows that MOF1-CDs@CC has good electrocatalytic performance toward EOR. To understand the final product, the CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC were obtained in KOH (1В вЂ‹M) containing acetaldehyde (1В вЂ‹M) or acetic acid (1В вЂ‹M) and the ОЅ value is 10В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1. As exhibited in Fig.В S12, the MOF1-CDs@CC shows higher catalytic performance for acetaldehyde, and no catalysis for acetic acid, indicating that one of the final products of EOR is acetic acid, which can also be verified by ultraviolet (UV) spectra (Fig.В S11). And the catalytic performance comparison between MOF1-CDs@CC and other Ni-based catalysts reported in the literatures was listed in TableВ 1 .

To optimize the concentration of electrolyte, the impact of KOH and EtOH concentrations toward EOR was studied through CV tests. The gradual increase of the j value followed by the change of KOH concentration is attributed to that the increase of KOH concentration improves the conductivity of the electrolyte, as exhibited in Fig.В 3b. Meanwhile, the increase of j value tends to be gentle when the concentration of KOH exceeds 1В вЂ‹M.

When the concentration of KOH remains unchanged, the concentration of ethanol also increases in the range of 0.1В вЂ‹MВ вЂ‹в€’В вЂ‹1В вЂ‹M, Fig.В 3c shows that the j value of EOR catalyzed by MOF1-CDs@CC rises gradually, which is due to that the increase of the EtOH concentration enhances the concentration of EtOH adsorbed by the surface of MOF1-CDs@CC catalyst, which contributes to the conversion of ethanol, thereby increasing the j value.

At the same time, the j value increases slowly while EtOH's concentration exceeds 1В вЂ‹M. Based on the principle of economy, the final composition of the electrolyte is composed of 1В вЂ‹M KOH +1В вЂ‹M EtOH mixed solution.

For comparison, the CV tests of samples (MOF 1, bare CC and MOF1-CDs@CC) were conducted at the same condition. As exhibited in Fig.В 3d, the j value of MOF1-CDs@CC is the highest, which indicated that CDs can promote the catalytic performance of MOF 1. According to the literature [41], CDs can enhance the adsorption of EtOH on the surface of catalyst and charge transfer in the EOR process. To verify whether this hypothesis is applicable to this work, some comparative tests were investigated. The peak j value of anode and cathode also increases as the scan rate rises, as exhibited in Fig.В 4 aвЂ“b. Meanwhile, the peak j value is positively related to the square root of the ОЅ value, indicating that the Ni2+/Ni3+ process is diffusion-controlled. And the diffusion coefficient (D) can be calculated by the equation:
j = 2.6 Г—
10 5
n
2 / 3
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2
C 0
, and it is worth mentioning that n and C0 stand for the number of electrons transferred and is initial concentration of the solution. As exhibited in Fig.В 4cвЂ“d, the slope of the plot of j vs ОЅ1/2 for MOF1-CDs@CC is higher than that of MOF 1, which indicates that the D value of MOF1-CDs@CC is superior to that of MOF 1 after introducing CDs.

To measure the effect of temperature on the reaction rate, the catalytic performance of MOF1-CDs@CC to oxidation of ethanol was measured in 1В вЂ‹M KOH at different temperatures. It can be seen from Fig.В 4e that the current density increases with the increase of reaction temperature, which is mainly attributed to the fact that temperature promotes the kinetics of the reaction. Meanwhile, Arrhenius curves of MOF1-CDs@CC catalyst were drawn between 1.0V and 1.6V, as shown in Fig.В 4f.

In addition, based on the Arrhenius equation of
log
j = log
A в€’
E a
/ 2.3 R T ,
where A and R are constants, the apparent activation energies were calculated at different potentials, as listed in TableВ 2 . However, when EtOH added to the electrolyte, the j value doesn't change much as the value of ОЅ increases (Fig.В S13).

To evaluate the catalytic kinetics of catalysts, EIS measurements of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC were performed. As illustrated in Fig.В 5 a, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and solution resistance (Rs) follow the trend of MOF 1>MOF 1-CDs@CC, which indicates that the addition of CDs can improve the surface electron transfer rate of the catalyst. Moreover, the influence of the amounts of CDs on the EOR was studied in varying concentrations of CD. The j value reaches the maximum with the concentration of CDs is 2В вЂ‹mgВ вЂ‹cmв€’3, as presented in Fig.В S14.

In addition, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) is also an important indicator for evaluating the activity of catalysts. Hence, the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC were carried out by CV tests from 1.20 to 1.28В вЂ‹V at various ОЅ values. As exhibited in Fig.В 5bвЂ“c, the curves of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC show a rectangular shape, indicating that no faradaic process occurred in this voltage range. Meanwhile, it can be obtained from Fig.В 5d that the Cdl value (11.62В вЂ‹mFВ вЂ‹cmв€’2) of MOF1-CDs@CC is higher than that of MOF 1 (6.32В вЂ‹mFВ вЂ‹cmв€’2). Based on the equation so MOF1-CDs@CC has higher electrocatalytic performance.
ECSAВ вЂ‹ =
C
d l
C s
( CsВ вЂ‹isВ вЂ‹constant )
,

To further evaluate the activities of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC, LSV curves of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC for the EOR were measured at the ОЅ value of 1В вЂ‹mVВ вЂ‹sв€’1. As illustrated in Fig.В S15, the onset potential of MOF1-CDs@CC is lower than that of MOF 1, which indicates that MOF1-CDs@CC can effectively reduce the electrode energy loss and increase the output energy.

The Tafel slope and the exchange current density (I0) are used to further evaluate the performance of catalysts. It can be seen from Fig.В 6 a that the Tafel slope (93.9В вЂ‹mVВ·decв€’1) of MOF1-CDs@CC is lower than that of MOF 1 (106В вЂ‹mVВ·decв€’1), which indicates that MOF1-CDs@CC has superior intrinsic activity. According to the Tafel formula of
О· =
E 0
в€’ E =
в€’ b l o g
(
I 0
)
+ log
(
I k
)
, The I
0 value of MOF1-CDs@CC is 2.06В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹10в€’5В вЂ‹AВ вЂ‹cmв€’2, which is superior to that of MOF 1 (1.89В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹10в€’5В вЂ‹AВ вЂ‹cmв€’2) [42]. Among them, b, E
0 and E stand for the Tafel slope, the equilibrium potential of EOR and the experimentally measured potential, respectively. All these results indicated that MOF1-CDs@CC is a promising catalyst for EOR.

In addition, at different j values the comparison of driving potential of MOF 1 and MOF1-CDs@CC for the EOR is shown in Fig.В 6b. It can be seen that the driving potential of MOF1-CDs@CC is lower than that of MOF 1В вЂ‹at any j values, and MOF1-CDs@CC showed more significant potential advantage than MOF 1В вЂ‹at high j value, indicating that MOF1-CDs@CC has great advantage in working at high j value than MOF 1 under the same conditions.

Stability is very important for the practical application of the catalyst. In the electrolyte of 1В вЂ‹M KOH +1В вЂ‹M EtOH, CA tests were carried out. The j value of MOF1-CDs@CC decreased from 119В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 to 100В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 after about 3В вЂ‹h durability test (Fig.В 6c). Meanwhile, the CV curves of MOF1-CDs@CC in the 1st cycle and 1001 st cycle in a fresh electrolyte (1В вЂ‹M KOHВ вЂ‹+В вЂ‹1В вЂ‹M EtOH) were carried out. The retention rate of the j value of MOF1-CDs@CC is 90% of that of the original one after 1000 cycles (Fig.В 6d), which indicated that MOF1-CDs@CC has good stability.

In summary, 2D Ni-MOF has been successfully constructed under solvothermal method. Then, MOF1-CDs@CC was successfully synthesised through wetness impregnation. Under alkaline condition, the optimal MOF1-CDs@CC exhibited a higher j value of 119В вЂ‹mAВ вЂ‹cmв€’2 at 1.6В вЂ‹V, and has a smaller Tafel slope of 93.9В вЂ‹mV decв€’1 and a larger exchange j value of 2.06В вЂ‹Г—В вЂ‹10в€’5В вЂ‹AВ вЂ‹cmв€’2. The excellent catalytic performance of MOF1-CDs@CC is mainly attributed to that CDs promote the adsorption of EtOH on the surface of the catalyst and enhance the rate of charge transfer after the introduction of CDs. Meanwhile, the durability test indicated that MOF 1-CDs@CC has excellent stability for EOR. This work provides a new idea for the application of MOFs in electrochemistry.
Xiaoyu Chi: Writing вЂ“ original draft. Lingling Gao: Formal analysis. Yujuan Zhang: Supervision. Tuoping Hu: Supervision, Writing вЂ“ review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this work by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 21676258), Central Leading Local Science and Technology Development Special Fund Project (YDZX20191400002636) and Shanxi Key Laboratory of Advanced Carbon Electrode Materials (202104010910019).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

CCDC 2099629 contains the supplementary crystallographic data for MOF 1. These data can be obtained free of charge via http://www.ccdc.cam.ac.uk/conts/retrieving.html, or from the Cambridge Crystallographic Data Centre, 12 Union Road, Cambridge CB2 1EZ, UK; fax: (+44) 1223-336-033; or e-mail: deposit@ccdc.cam.ac.uk.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jssc.2022.123094.
